title: Testing glaze on test tiles
subtitle: Cutting bisque test tiles, applying glaze in graduated thickness, and recording results
excerpt: A test tile is a small slab of bisqueware used to preview how a glaze will behave at full kiln temperature without risking a finished piece. This tutorial covers cutting and bisque-firing the tiles, applying a glaze in two or three thickness bands per tile, firing alongside the studio's normal glaze work, and recording the result against the original mix so the next batch can be adjusted.
sourceNotes: Test tile protocol derived from first principles and standard studio practice. Encyclopaedia Britannica, eleventh edition (1911), ceramics entry, on the historical development of glaze testing. Orton Ceramic Foundation cone reference.

glossaryTerms.definition: Pottery fired once at cone 06 (999°C). Hard and porous; takes glaze for the second firing.
Unfired clay in any drying stage; ready for the bisque firing once bone-dry.
Air-dried but unfired clay. The stage at which greenware enters the bisque firing.
The second kiln firing at the body's mature temperature (cone 6 / 1222°C for stoneware) that melts the glaze and vitrifies the body.
The density of a mixed glaze relative to water. Most dipping glazes work between 1.40 and 1.55.

content.text: A test tile is a small flat slab of bisqueware that previews how a glaze will fire at full temperature on the studio's stoneware body. Test tiles cost almost nothing to make, fire alongside the regular glaze work without taking extra time, and avoid the cost of glazing a full piece with a glaze that turns out wrong. Anyone glazing should keep a stack of fired tiles labelled with the glaze name, the thickness applied, the cone, and the date. Both bisque and glaze firing in a kiln are required to make and use test tiles.

attrs.title: Silica + kiln safety
attrs.body: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard and silicosis is irreversible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powder is open. Wet-clean only; no sweeping. Greenware tile sanding happens outdoors with a mask, not in a kitchen-air-shared space. For the kiln firings: ventilation by downdraft vent or by an open window with an extractor. Never open a hot kiln below 80°C. Keep a Class A fire extinguisher within reach of the studio area.

content.text: Cutting the tiles

Roll out a slab of smooth stoneware 6-7 mm thick using a pottery rolling pin and slab guides. Cut the slab into rectangles with a fettling knife and a ruler. The standard test tile is 100 mm by 50 mm; large enough to apply two or three glaze thickness bands, small enough to fit many into the kiln in any spare space.

Score a 10 mm strip across the top of each tile with the needle tool to mark the unglazed handling area; this is where the glaze label will be scratched in (the unglazed clay takes a pencil or scratched mark, glaze does not). Pierce a 5 mm hanging hole in the top of each tile with the needle tool; fired tiles can be threaded on a string for storage.
Cut a dozen or two tiles at a time; they bisque-fire as a group and last for many testing sessions. Set the cut tiles on a wooden board and dry slowly to
bone-dry
over two to four days, flipping daily so they dry evenly and stay flat. Warped tiles do not test glaze fairly.
Bisque firing the tiles
Load the bone-dry
greenware
tiles flat on a kiln-washed shelf, stacked one on top of another; tiles can share a load with no penalty. Fire to cone 06 (999°C) using a slow climb through the early hours. The fired
bisqueware
tiles are hard, slightly porous, and ready for glazing.
Applying glaze in graduated thickness

Most glazes look different at different thicknesses. The studio needs to see thin, medium, and thick coats of the same glaze on the same tile to know what range will work on a finished piece. The technique is to dip the tile in stages.
1. The triple-dip method

Stir the glaze thoroughly. Hold the bisque tile by the unglazed top strip; dip the lower two-thirds into the glaze bucket for two seconds; lift and let drain. The lower two-thirds now has a single coat of glaze.

Allow the first coat to dry to the touch; usually a minute or two. Dip the lower one-third into the glaze for two more seconds; lift and drain. The lower third now has two coats; the middle third has one coat; the top strip is unglazed.

Allow the second coat to dry. Dip the very bottom (the lowest 20-30 mm) for two more seconds; lift and drain. The bottom strip now has three coats; the next strip up has two; the strip above that has one; the top strip is unglazed. Three thickness bands plus the bare bisque give four data points on a single tile.
2. Marking the tile
Before the glaze is fully dry, scratch the glaze label into the unglazed top strip with the needle tool. Include: glaze name, the
specific gravity
of the mix if known, the cone, the firing date (or batch number). Scratch firmly; the marks must be visible after the glaze fires.
Glaze firing
Tiles fire in any spare space inside a regular
glaze firing

. Set the tile flat on a kiln-washed shelf with at least 25 mm of clearance from any neighbouring work; a glaze that runs unexpectedly should not contaminate a finished piece. Tiles fire alongside whatever else is going in the kiln; do not run a kiln of test tiles only.

Fire to the cone target of the glaze being tested (cone 6 (1222°C) for mid-fire glazes) following the kiln manufacturer's standard schedule. Cool with the lid closed until 80°C or below before opening.
Reading the result

Lay the fired tile on a clean white surface in daylight. Check the colour shifts between the three bands. Some glazes go clear at thin, solid at thick. Look at the break colour at edges where the glaze runs and pools at the bottom of each band. Spot any crawling, pinholing, or crazing (faults that did not show in the wet glaze). Note the surface feel (matte, satin, glossy).

Compare the tile against the original mix specifications. If the glaze is too matte, the silica or flux balance needs adjusting; if it crazes, the thermal expansion is too high for the body; if it pinholes, the firing climb may need slowing through the bisque-burnout phase. The tile result becomes a reference for the next batch.
Storing and retrieving the tiles

Thread the fired tiles onto a string or stack on a shelf indexed by glaze name. The library of fired tiles becomes the studio reference; new glazes get tested before going onto a finished piece; existing glazes get re-tested when the mix changes (a new bag of dry powder, a different supplier, a different mixing day). Pin the tile string near the glazing area so the reference is in sight when choosing a glaze for a finished piece.
What it looks like when it is right

A well-made test tile shows three clear glaze-thickness bands, each band even and flat without runs onto the kiln shelf. The bare bisque strip at the top carries a legible scratched label. The tile sits flat on the bench without rocking, meaning the bisque was fired flat. The glaze surface across the tile is consistent: no patchy crawl, no unexpected colour streaking.
Common mistakes
Forgetting to label the tile. An unlabelled fired tile is useless; the studio cannot tell from looking which glaze it tested. Scratch the label before firing.
Tile warps in the bisque firing because of uneven drying. The warped tile gives an uneven glaze-thickness reading across each band. Flip greenware tiles daily during drying to keep them flat.

Single dip only, no graduated thickness. A single-thickness tile does not show how the glaze changes with coat weight, which is one of the main things the test is meant to reveal. Always apply at least two bands; three is the working standard.
Testing on porcelain when the studio body is stoneware. Different clay bodies produce different glaze surfaces from the same glaze. Test on the body the studio actually uses.

items.symptom: Tile cracks in the bisque firing
items.cause: Greenware tile was not fully bone-dry, or dried unevenly and warped
items.fix: Dry tiles flat for four days and flip daily; check tile temperature against the cheek before bisque loading (cool means residual moisture)

items.symptom: Glaze runs off the tile onto the kiln shelf
items.cause: Glaze too fluxed, or coat applied too thick at the bottom band
items.fix: Set tiles on a small offcut of kiln furniture so any runs catch on the offcut, not the shelf; reduce the third-coat dip time to one second

items.symptom: Scratched label is invisible after firing
items.cause: Scratch was too light, or marked into glaze rather than into bare bisque
items.fix: Scratch firmly into the bare top strip with the needle tool. Press deep enough to feel a groove. The bare bisque keeps the mark through firing. Scratches on glaze melt smooth

items.symptom: Tile gives an unreadable result (colours streak or smear)
items.cause: Glaze was not stirred properly, or tile was contaminated with dust
items.fix: Stir glaze to full suspension before dipping; wipe bisque tile with a damp sponge before glazing
